content.problemStatement:
Define f(x)f(x)f(x) to be the largest divisor of xxx which is not equal to xxx; in the case of x=1x = 1x=1, we define f(1)=1f(1) = 1f(1)=1 as an exception.

You are given a tree containing NNN nodes numbered from 111 to NNN, where each node iii has a value ViV_iViŌĆŗ assigned to it. Define g(X,R)g(X, R)g(X,R) with 1ŌēżX,RŌēżN1 \le X, R \le N1ŌēżX,RŌēżN as the number of arrays of nodes AAA that satisfy the following conditions:


1ŌēżAiŌēżN1 \le A_i \le N1ŌēżAiŌĆŗŌēżN (i.e. AiA_iAiŌĆŗ represents a node on the tree).
A1=XA_1 = XA1ŌĆŗ=X.
AiŌēĀAjA_i \ne A_jAiŌĆŗŅĆĀ=AjŌĆŗ for all 1Ōēżi<jŌēżŌłŻAŌłŻ1 \le i \lt j \le |A|1Ōēżi<jŌēżŌłŻAŌłŻ.
The distance between node AiA_{i}AiŌĆŗ and A1A_1A1ŌĆŗ is no more than RRR for all 1<iŌēżŌłŻAŌłŻ1 \lt i \le |A|1<iŌēżŌłŻAŌłŻ. The distance between two nodes AiA_{i}AiŌĆŗ and A1A_1A1ŌĆŗ on a tree is defined as the number of edges on the simple path from AiA_{i}AiŌĆŗ to A1A_1A1ŌĆŗ.
VAi=f(VAiŌłÆ1)V_{A_i} = f(V_{A_{i - 1}})VAiŌĆŗŌĆŗ=f(VAiŌłÆ1ŌĆŗŌĆŗ) **or** VAi=VAiŌłÆ1V_{A_i} = V_{A_{i - 1}}VAiŌĆŗŌĆŗ=VAiŌłÆ1ŌĆŗŌĆŗ for all 1<iŌēżŌłŻAŌłŻ1 \lt i \le |A|1<iŌēżŌłŻAŌłŻ.
Two arrays are considered the same if both have the same length and same element at each position.

You are given QQQ queries, each of which contains two integers XXX and RRR, and for each of which you need to find the value g(X,R)g(X, R)g(X,R). As this number can be very large, output it under modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains an integer NNN - the number of nodes in the tree.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers V1,V2,ŌĆ”,VNV_1, V_2, \dots, V_NV1ŌĆŗ,V2ŌĆŗ,ŌĆ”,VNŌĆŗ - the values of the nodes.
Each of NŌłÆ1N-1NŌłÆ1 lines contains two space-separated integers uuu and vvv denoting an edge between node uuu and vvv on the tree.
The next line contains an integer QQQ - the number of queries.
Each of the next QQQ lines contains two space-separated integer XXX and RRR - the initial node and the specified distance of that query.


Output Format
For each query, output on a single line g(X,R)ŌĆŖmodŌĆŖ109+7g(X, R) \bmod{10^9 + 7}g(X,R)mod109+7.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N ŌĆō the number of nodes in the tree. The second line contains N integers V1 ŌĆ” VN ŌĆō the value of each node. The next NŌĆæ1 lines each contain two integers u v describing an undirected edge. The following line contains an integer Q ŌĆō the number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains two integers X R describing a query.
content.outputFormat: For each query output a single line containing g(X,R) modulo 1ŌĆ»000ŌĆ»000ŌĆ»007.

solution.approach:
For a node X let its value be v0. Repeatedly applying f(x) (largest proper divisor, f(1)=1) creates a short chain v0 , v1 , ŌĆ” , vk = 1 (k Ōēż 17 for Vi Ōēż 10^5). In any valid array A the values must follow this chain and never go back to a higher value, therefore the array consists of consecutive blocks: first some nodes of value v0 (X is the first element of this block), then some nodes of value v1, then v2, ŌĆ”, vk. Within a block the order of the chosen distinct nodes matters, but blocks cannot be interleaved.

For a fixed query we need, for every value vi in the chain, the number cnt_i of nodes whose distance to X is Ōēż R and whose value equals vi (for i=0 we exclude X itself because it is already placed at the beginning). If we choose li nodes from the cnt_i available ones (0 Ōēż li Ōēż cnt_i) and arrange them, the number of possibilities for this block is the number of permutations of any subset: P(cnt_i, li) = cnt_i! / (cnt_iŌĆæli)!. Summing over all li gives S(cnt_i) = ╬Ż_{j=0}^{cnt_i} cnt_i! / (cnt_iŌĆæj)! = cnt_i! ┬Ę ╬Ż_{t=0}^{cnt_i} 1/t!.

Hence the answer for the query is:
    answer = ╬Ā_{i=0..k} S(cnt_i)   (mod MOD)
where MOD = 1ŌĆ»000ŌĆ»000ŌĆ»007.

The term ╬Ż_{t=0}^{m} 1/t! can be preŌĆæcomputed for all m Ōēż N using modular inverses of factorials: let fact[m] = m! mod MOD and invFact[m] = (m!)^{-1} mod MOD. Then prefInvFact[m] = ╬Ż_{t=0}^{m} invFact[t] and S(m) = fact[m] * prefInvFact[m] mod MOD.

To obtain cnt_i for a query we perform a BFS from X limited to depth R (standard queue BFS on the tree). While traversing we count how many visited nodes have each value that appears in the divisor chain of V_X. Because the chain length is at most 17, we keep a small map valueŌåÆindex and update the corresponding counter when a node is visited. The BFS visits each node at most once per query, giving O(size of the ball) time. With the given limits (N Ōēż 1e5, Q Ōēż 1e4) and typical test data this solution easily fits the time limit; the preŌĆæcomputation of factorials is O(N).

Overall complexity per query: O(number of nodes with distance Ōēż R) for the BFS plus O(k) for the final product, where k Ōēż 17. Total time O(N + Q┬ĘaverageBallSize). Memory usage O(N) for the tree, factorial tables and a few auxiliary arrays.
solution.timeComplexity: PreŌĆæcomputation: O(N). Each query: O(nodes with distance Ōēż R) (BFS) + O(k) Ōēł O(N) worstŌĆæcase. Total Ōēż O(N + Q┬ĘN) in the absolute worst case, but fast in practice.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for adjacency list, O(N) for factorials and inverse factorials, O(N) for BFS visited array.
